bids.reasoning: This is the opening offer at the very beginning of the negotiation (t=0.0). I will bid my most preferred value for every issue to establish a strong anchor and claim maximum utility (1.0). I can evaluate the opponent's preferences and find mutually beneficial concessions in later rounds based on their counteroffers.

The opponent's message uses an early aggressive threat tactic which I will disregard as cheap talk, especially at t=0.02. To keep the negotiation moving and show willingness to deal, I am making a small concession on my lowest-weight issue (issueA), moving from valueD to valueC, while holding firm on all other issues since they are much more important to my overall utility.

Early in the negotiation (t=0.04). The opponent is repeating their opening offer and using aggressive "cheap talk" threats to force early capitulation on my part. I will ignore these empty threats, as they are non-binding tactics, but I will make another minor concession on my lowest-priority issue (issueA) to demonstrate flexibility and keep the negotiation fluid, while holding firm on my most important issues (issueC and issueD).

The negotiation is still very early (t=0.06) but the opponent is repeatedly making the exact same aggressive offer and issuing cheap-talk threats. I will ignore the threats and continue conceding systematically on my least important issues to probe for flexibility. By fully conceding `issueA` to `valueA`, which the opponent has demanded, I'm making a clear compromise while preserving maximum utility on my highly weighted issues `issueC` and `issueD`.

I am still very early in the negotiation (t=0.08). The opponent repeatedly sends the same offer giving me minimal utility along with aggressive empty threats that I will ignore as cheap talk. I have fully conceded on my lowest-priority issue, issueA, to show a willingness to cooperate, but the opponent has not budged. I will now make a small concession on my next lowest-priority issue, issueB, dropping it from valueD to valueC, while keeping issueC and issueD at valueD to protect my main sources of utility.

The negotiation is still very early (t=0.1), so there is no rush to accept the opponent's lopsided offer despite their aggressive cheap-talk threats about walking away. The opponent finally showed slight movement on issueD, acknowledging that compromises can be made. I will continue my pattern of gradual concessions on lower-weight issues (issueA and issueB) while holding firm on my most highly valued issues (issueC and issueD).

The current time is very early (t=0.12). The opponent's aggressive threats to walk away are mere cheap-talk tactics intended to rush me, so I will ignore them and focus on the bids. The opponent has continuously asked for valueA on issueA and issueB, and I have already conceded significant ground on these lower-weight issues. I will now offer valueA for both issueA and issueB to meet their demands on their likely high-priority issues, while holding firm on valueD for issueC and issueD, which carry the majority of my utility weight (0.7 combined).

The opponent is aggressively employing fake urgency and threats in an attempt to coerce concessions, demonstrating almost no movement aside from a minor shift on issueD. As the time fraction is still very early (t=0.14), I will ignore these untrusted cheap-talk threats. I have already fully conceded on issueA and issueB, which hold my lowest weights. To show continued willingness to negotiate while protecting my most critical issues, I will lightly concede on issueC (from valueD to valueC) while holding firm on issueD, maintaining a solid utility around 0.68.

The opponent is aggressively using cheap talk, threats of "blacklisting," and fake urgency to force concessions, yet their bidding behavior shows slow but steady movement (offering valueB on issueD, and now valueB on issueC). Since we are still very early in the negotiation (t=0.16), I will ignore the empty threats and protect my most crucial issue, issueD, by keeping it at valueD. I will offer a moderate combination on issueB and issueC to explore their preferences while maintaining a strong utility for myself.

The opponent is aggressively using cheap-talk threats ("blacklisting", "walking away") and fake urgency, even though the current time fraction is only 0.18. They have also regressed on their minor concessions for issue C and D, while demanding maximum utility. To protect my utility, I will disregard their hostile messages and hold firm on my most critical issues (C and D), making a minor adjustment to my previous offers on issues A and B to maintain a reasonable utility score well above 0.75.

The opponent is aggressively demanding I accept their lowball offers, using fabricated threats of blacklisting which I can recognize as a negotiation tactic (cheap talk). It's still early in the negotiation (t=0.20), so I have no reason to concede heavily. I will maintain a strong stance on my high-value issues (C and D) while offering slight, strategic concessions on my low-value issues (A and B) to slowly test their true preferences.

I see the opponent has yielded slightly on issueB by offering valueB, so I will incorporate that into my bid. I am maintaining my rigid stance on issueD, my most critical issue, while tentatively conceding to valueC on issueC to show a willingness to compromise. The opponent's aggressive messages and fake time constraints are recognized as cheap talk, so I will hold my ground on my highest-priority issues since we are only at t=0.22.

I have analyzed the opponent's bidding history and observed that they consistently hold firm on `issueA` while showing a willingness to concede on `issueD` and occasionally on `issueB` and `issueC`. Since `issueD` is my highest-weighted priority and `issueA` is my lowest, our preferences are largely complementary. I will disregard the opponent's aggressive messages as cheap talk, and I will propose a competitive counter-offer that gives them their preferred value on `issueA` while securing maximum value for my highest priority.

The opponent's aggressive threats in the message history are just cheap talk and do not match the deadline (t=0.26), so I will ignore the bluster. They recently showed flexibility on issueD (offering valueC) and issueB (offering valueB), so I will tailor my counteroffer to meet these signals. I will offer valueC on issueD and valueB on issueB while securing valueD on issueC, maintaining a solid utility around 0.70 for myself while exploring alternative trade-offs they might accept.

I am targeting a utility of 0.73, making measured mid-game concessions while ignoring the opponent's consistently aggressive threats and fake urgency as simple negotiation tactics. The opponent's last offer showed a willingness to concede on issueC to valueC, so I will match that while asking for valueB on issueB (which they previously offered at t=0.20) and holding firm on issueD, my highest-utility issue.

The opponent's messages consist of a steady stream of aggressive threats and cheap talk about "blacklisting" me or walking away, all of which are disregarded as non-binding tactics. However, looking at their actual bidding behavior, they have recently shown flexibility on issueA, issueC, and issueD through standalone concessions. To approach an agreement while still maintaining a strong utility early on (t=0.30), I am adopting their latest concession on issueA (valueB) and matching their preference on issueB (valueA). In return, I am holding firm on issueD and proposing a mid-range value for issueC, targeting an overall utility of ~0.71.

The opponent's persistent hostile messages are standard negotiation tactics and do not reflect the actual time fraction (t=0.32) or binding constraints, so I will treat them as cheap talk. Their bidding behavior indicates a willingness to explore different configurations, recently conceding slightly on issueB and issueD. I will reciprocate their latest move on issueB while maintaining high utility values for my most critical issues (issueD and issueC) to maintain a steady utility level of approximately 0.76.

I am ignoring the opponent's repeated, fake threats to walk away and blacklist me, treating them as cheap talk. Since they recently offered `valueB` for issueC, I will accept that concession on this moderately weighted issue and pair it with `valueA` for issueA, while holding firm on my highest-priority issue D (`valueD`) and maintaining a solid value for B (`valueC`). This strategy keeps my utility comfortably above 0.70 while systematically exploring potential compromises.

The opponent continues to make empty threats about walking away, which we can safely ignore as a negotiation tactic, especially given that it is still relatively early in the timeline (t=0.36). Their latest offer conceded to valueC for issueC, which is one of my highly weighted issues, indicating we are making progress there. Given my high weights on issueC and issueD, I will hold firm on valueD for issueD and valueC for issueC, while offering slight concessions on the lower-weighted issues A and B to encourage further engagement.

I will ignore the opponent's aggressive messages, as they are a clear negotiation tactic using fake ultimatums while their numbers show active concessions (like recently offering valueC on issueB). My goal is high utility on issueD (weight 0.4) and issueC (weight 0.3). I will concede to their obvious preference on issueA (valueA) and recent concession on issueB (valueC), while maintaining my highest utilities on issueC and issueD to keep my overall expected value near 0.8.

The opponent's repetitive aggressive messages containing threats to "blacklist" or "walk away" are recognized as cheap talk and fake urgency, particularly since we are only at t=0.4 in the negotiation. The opponent's offers, however, indicate steady but small concessions across all issues, exploring different combinations such as offering `valueC` on my highest weight issue (`issueD`) and `valueB` on `issueB`. My utility is heavily dependent on `issueD` and `issueC`, so I will stand firm on those by maintaining them at `valueD`, while conceding significantly on `issueA` and `issueB` where my weights are the lowest, aiming for a utility of around 0.79 to show cooperative flexibility without compromising core targets mid-game.

I am ignoring the opponent's repeated and identically phrased aggressive threats, treating them simply as a cheap talk tactic. At this mid-game stage (t=0.42), maintaining my utility above 0.75 remains a priority. The opponent's bidding history reveals an occasional flexibility for `valueC` on issue D (e.g., at t=0.24 and t=0.40) and `valueC` on issue B (t=0.38), so my bid uses those values while giving them their clear favorite on issue A and holding firm on issue C.

The opponent's repetitive, aggressive threats to walk away and blacklist me are treated as cheap talk and negotiation tactics. Their bids, however, show a strong preference for `valueA` and `valueB` on `issueA` and `issueB`. Knowing that these two issues have the lowest importance to me (weights 0.1 and 0.2 respectively), I will concede on them while maintaining my highest utility values on `issueC` and `issueD` to preserve a favorable utility (around 0.75).

The opponent's messages consist entirely of aggressive boilerplate threats, which I am disregarding in favor of analyzing their actual bids. I am approaching the mid-game (t=0.46), so I will try offering a combination from previous rounds where I concede on the less important issues (issueA and issueB) while maintaining high values on my most important issues (issueC and issueD). I will incorporate their recent bid of `valueC` on `issueB`, combining it with `valueC` on `issueC` and `valueD` on `issueD` to target a utility of around 0.78.

The opponent makes persistent hostile threats in their messages, which I am disregarding as cheap talk designed to force premature concessions. Given the time fraction (0.48) and the opponent's latest offer focusing on issueA at `valueC` and issueB at `valueA`, I will offer exactly these values for those lower-importance issues. In return, I will hold firm on my highest-importance issues (issueC and issueD) at `valueD`, maintaining a strong utility over 0.8 while matching their recent concessions.

The current time fraction is 0.5, so we are at the halfway point. The opponent's message history is entirely comprised of aggressive, non-binding cheap talk claiming they will walk away or blacklist me; as their underlying bidding patterns are still showing slight concessions, I will ignore their threats completely. To maintain a strong position while testing the waters for an agreement, I am holding completely firm on issueC and issueD (which are my most important issues with weights 0.3 and 0.4 respectively) but offering a configuration on issueA and issueB that concedes from my maximum to better accommodate them.

I am ignoring the opponent's aggressive threats and blacklisting comments, seeing them as cheap talk intended to rush a concession. The opponent has finally shown substantial movement on issueB by proposing valueD in their latest bid. I will lock in their concession on issueB (valueD) and offer valueA for issueA, which they strongly prefer, while keeping my most critical issues (issueC and issueD) at higher values to maintain a profitable utility margin around 0.84.

The opponent continues to rely on cheap talk, making empty threats about walkaways and blacklisting, but their bidding behavior shows willingness to concede on `issueB`, where they just offered `valueC`. Since my highest priorities are `issueD` and `issueC`, I will hold firm on them. I will meet the opponent on `issueA: valueA` and `issueB: valueC` to signal cooperation while maintaining a high overall utility of ~0.866.

The opponent's repetitive messages are aggressive but follow a clear formula of threats ("final offer, I'll blacklist you"), which function as cheap talk that I will ignore since the current time fraction is only 0.56. I will keep my weight focused on my highest valued issue (issueD), maintaining it at valueD. Since the opponent has shown a willingness to concede on issueB to valueD (at t=0.52) and issueC to valueB (at t=0.54), I will incorporate these specific values alongside their preferred value for issueA to form a mutually workable compromise that preserves a utility of ~0.76 for me.

The opponent is aggressively using cheap talk, repeatedly threatening to walk away unless I immediately accept, yet their offers contradict these claims as they continue to slowly concede. Most recently, they offered `valueD` for `issueB` and `valueB` for `issueD`. I will disregard their hostile bluffing and capitalize on the opening by incorporating their `issueB: valueD` offer. To reciprocate and move towards an agreement, I will fully concede `issueA` (my lowest priority) to `valueA` and step down from my absolute ideal on `issueD` to `valueC` (a value they previously proposed), while maintaining a solid utility buffer for myself.

The opponent's repetitive aggressive messages and threats to walk away, combined with fake urgency, are clear negotiation tactics that I will ignore. Looking at the offer history, the opponent has shown consistent preference for issueA: valueA and recent flexibility on issueB: valueD. I am offering a compromise that matches their preferences on issueA and issueB, while making a partial concession on my most valuable issue (issueD) to valueC, maintaining a solid utility of approximately 0.81 for myself.

The opponent's messages consist of repetitive, aggressive threats which appear to be automated cheap-talk tactics; I will ignore them and focus purely on their bidding patterns. In their latest offer, they proposed valueC for both issueB and issueC, showing a willingness to move on these fronts. Therefore, I will match their exact values for issueA, issueB, and issueC, whilst standing firm on issueD (my highest weight issue) at my top value, yielding a solid 0.785 utility while offering significant common ground.

The opponent's constant threats to "walk away" and warnings of "blacklisting" are aggressive negotiation tactics and obvious cheap talk, which I will ignore. Their bidding behavior shows they are willing to accept `valueC` for `issueA` and `issueC`, as seen in their most recent offer, and they have offered `valueC` for `issueB` recently as well. I will construct an offer that matches these preferences on the three lowest-weighted issues to encourage agreement, while firmly maintaining `valueD` on my top issue, `issueD`, preserving a solid overall utility of approximately 0.838.

The opponent is aggressively employing a cheap-talk threat strategy with repeated fake walkaway ultimatums, but their actual offers show typical concession behavior, periodically testing valueD on my low-weight issueB and valueB on my high-weight issueD. I will ignore the bluffs and continue to seek an optimal compromise in the 0.7 range by matching some of their recent values while holding strong on my highest weights. I am now matching their proposed values for issues A, B, and D from recent turns to show alignment, while keeping issueC at my highest utility value to secure an acceptable outcome before the final deadline.

The opponent's hostile messages are recurring aggressive tactics trying to force immediate concession, but I must base my strategy strictly on their actual bidding history. Examining their recent offers, they proposed B=valueD at t=0.66 and C=valueC, D=valueC at t=0.68. I will construct a bid mixing these identified acceptable values (A=valueA, B=valueD, C=valueC, D=valueC), netting me a solid utility of approximately 0.73, which appropriately reflects the mid-late stage (t=0.68) of the negotiation while presenting them with terms they have individually suggested.

The opponent's messages consist of identical bluffing threats to walk away, which I will ignore as cheap talk since they continue proposing new combinations. I will target a utility of approximately 0.78 by accepting the opponent's willingness to yield on issues B and C while insisting on valueD for my most important issue, issueD. This strategy balances making concessions on less critical issues while preserving utility where it matters most to me.

The opponent's negotiation style relies heavily on aggressive but empty threats ("I am walking away"), which I treat as cheap talk. By tracking their proposals, I see they have recently shown willingness to accept `valueC` across all issues at various times (e.g., A=C at t=0.72, B=C and C=C at t=0.68, D=C at t=0.68 and t=0.72). Proposing `valueC` for all issues yields a solid 0.73 utility for me and combines values they have independently validated, increasing the likelihood of agreement as we approach the deadline.

The opponent's repetitive aggressive messages are clear examples of cheap talk and do not align with their evolving offer behavior, which has demonstrated a willingness to yield on the highest-priority issue for me (`issueD` to `valueD`). By securing a high baseline utility on `issueD` and `issueC`, I can afford to concede somewhat on lower-weight issues like `issueA` and `issueB`. Therefore, I will respond with a counter-offer that adopts their proposed values for `issueB` and `issueA` while maintaining my preferred position on `issueC` and matching their concession on `issueD`.

The negotiation is well into the later stages at t=0.76. My opponent employs aggressive rhetoric in their messages, but their actual offers show a willingness to concede, particularly on issueD and issueB at t=0.74 and t=0.70. To target a utility around 0.73 for myself while offering a compromise based on the opponent's past proposals, I will combine values they have previously offered (issueB: valueC, issueC: valueB, issueD: valueD) and keep issueA at valueB.

I am targeting a utility around 0.75 as we approach the deadline. The opponent's last offer included values for issues A, B, and C that I can partially accommodate (valueC, valueC, and valueB, respectively), yielding acceptable utility for me. I will adopt their preferred values for those three lower-weight issues while firmly keeping my most important issue (issueD) at valueD to protect my overall score. I will ignore the opponent's aggressive threats, which have been a constant but empty tactic throughout the negotiation.

The opponent's most recent offer at t=0.80 provides me with a utility of 0.708. Given the advancing time (t=0.8), I'm willing to lower my utility targets, but I'll make a counteroffer that modifies their previous bid slightly while securing a better outcome for myself (0.761). I will accept their terms on issues B, C, and D, but shift issue A to valueC, which they have proposed in several of their earlier bids.

The negotiation is entering the late stage (t=0.82), and the opponent's aggressive messages are standard high-pressure tactics, which we can ignore in favor of observing their actual bidding behavior. The opponent recently offered `issueB: valueD` (t=0.80) and `issueB: valueC, issueC: valueC, issueD: valueC` (t=0.82). To compromise while maintaining a solid utility, I will accept their latest suggestions for issues A, B, and D, but assert my preference for `issueC: valueD` to keep my overall utility healthy (around 0.78).

The negotiation is in the late stages (t=0.84), and the opponent has started giving substantial value on my most important issue (issueD=valueD). Their last offer achieves approximately 0.71 utility for me. I will ignore their harsh, aggressive text prompts as cheap talk and instead propose a mutually acceptable compromise by shifting issueC slightly towards my preference (valueC) while holding the rest of their recent proposal steady, targeting around 0.78 utility.

The opponent's aggressive messages are treated as cheap talk, but their actual offers reveal flexibility across multiple issues. I will synthesize their recent preferences by taking `issueA: valueC` (from t=0.84, t=0.86), `issueB: valueD` (from t=0.86), and combination `issueC: valueC` with `issueD: valueC` (from t=0.82). This produces a balanced counter-offer that incorporates their exact recent requested values while maintaining a solid 0.78 utility for my side as we enter the closing phase.

The opponent's aggressive messages are pure cheap talk, as their bids reveal a willingness to compromise; at t=0.88 they offered `valueD` for `issueA`, `valueB` for `issueB`, and `valueC` for `issueC`. I will adopt these three values from their latest offer to demonstrate alignment while holding firm on `issueD` (`valueD`, which carries my highest weight of 0.4) to maintain my utility at a strong 0.81. This creates a highly cooperative offer that protects my most vital interest as the deadline nears.

The deadline is approaching (t=0.90), so I need to make a substantial offer that meets the opponent's needs while staying well above my reservation value. The opponent just offered `valueD` for both `issueA` and `issueC`, which are highly favorable for me, meaning they likely don't value them much or are using them as concessions. They seem to prioritize `issueB` and `issueD`, so I will adopt their concessions on A and C, offer a compromise of `valueB` on `issueB`, and `valueC` on `issueD` to reach a mutually agreeable state. I will ignore their threatening messaging, treating it as cheap talk.

The negotiation is in its final stages at t=0.92, and despite the opponent's repeated aggressive cheap-talk insisting on walking away, their bidding behavior shows ongoing concessions across various issues. Their latest offer (issueA: valueD, issueB: valueB, issueC: valueB, issueD: valueC) provides me a utility of 0.627. To prioritize reaching an agreement before the deadline while aiming for an acceptable utility >0.70, I will accept their values for issues A, B, and D, and only improve issueC to valueC.

The opponent's most recent offer (issueA: valueC, issueB: valueC, issueC: valueC, issueD: valueC) yields a utility of 0.73 for me, which is actually higher than the utility of my own previous offer (0.705). Since we are very close to the deadline (t=0.94) and this offer exceeds my recent utility targets, I will accept the opponent's terms by mirroring their offer to secure the agreement.
